course_info.title: Medical Terminology
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 3 hours per week

learning_objectives: Identify the three components of medical terms: root, prefix, suffix
Apply word-building rules to construct and deconstruct medical terms
Understand the Greek and Latin origins of medical vocabulary
readings: Medical Terminology: A Living Language Ch. 1
NLM Unified Medical Language System Overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Medical terminology is the language of healthcare. Mastery of this language is essential for every health informatics professional who must interpret clinical data, map terminologies, and ensure accurate coding.
key_concepts: Word roots: the core meaning of a medical term (e.g., cardi- = heart)
Prefixes modify the root (e.g., tachy- = fast, brady- = slow)
Suffixes indicate procedure, condition, or disease (e.g., -ectomy = removal)
Combining vowels connect roots and suffixes (e.g., the 'o' in cardiology)
Rules for building and deconstructing compound medical terms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interactive exercise building terms from components: gastroenterology, electrocardiogram, hypertension
setup_requirements: Medical terminology flashcard app or physical cards

review.value: The fundamental unit of meaning in a medical term, usually derived from Greek or Latin
A word root plus a combining vowel (usually 'o') used to link roots or connect to suffixes

learning_objectives: Identify body planes and body cavities
Describe the levels of structural organization in the body
readings: Medical Terminology: A Living Language Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: Before learning organ-specific terminology, we must master the universal language of anatomical position, direction, and body organization.
key_concepts: Anatomical position and its importance as a reference point
Directional terms: superior/inferior, anterior/posterior, medial/lateral, proximal/distal
Body planes: sagittal, coronal (frontal), transverse (axial)
Body cavities: dorsal (cranial, spinal), ventral (thoracic, abdominal, pelvic)
Levels of organization: chemical, cellular, tissue, organ, system, organism
lecture_notes.demonstration: Labeling exercise using anatomical diagrams to practice directional terms and body planes

review.value: Body standing erect, face forward, arms at sides with palms facing forward - the universal reference point
Vertical plane dividing the body into left and right portions

learning_objectives: Define terms related to the cardiovascular system
Define terms related to the respiratory system
Interpret common diagnostic and procedural terms for both systems
readings: Medical Terminology: A Living Language Ch. 5-6

lecture_notes.introduction: The cardiovascular and respiratory systems are among the most commonly documented in clinical records. Mastering their terminology is critical for accurate EHR documentation and coding.
key_concepts: Cardiovascular roots: cardi/o, angi/o, hem/o, vas/o, ather/o
Cardiovascular conditions: myocardial infarction, arrhythmia, atherosclerosis, hypertension
Respiratory roots: pulmon/o, pneum/o, bronch/o, thorac/o
Respiratory conditions: pneumonia, COPD, asthma, pneumothorax
Common procedures: angioplasty, CABG, bronchoscopy, thoracentesis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Reading and interpreting a sample cardiology consultation note, identifying all medical terms and their meanings

review.value: my/o (muscle) + cardi/o (heart) + -al (pertaining to) + infarction (tissue death from blood supply loss) = heart attack
bronch/o (bronchus) + -scopy (visual examination) = visual examination of the bronchial tubes using a scope

learning_objectives: Define terms related to the musculoskeletal system
Define terms related to the nervous system
Interpret orthopedic and neurological diagnostic terms
readings: Medical Terminology: A Living Language Ch. 9-10

lecture_notes.introduction: The musculoskeletal and nervous systems have rich, specialized vocabularies. These terms appear frequently in clinical documentation, rehabilitation records, and neurology reports.
key_concepts: Musculoskeletal roots: oste/o, arthr/o, my/o, chondr/o, tend/o
Conditions: osteoporosis, arthritis, tendinitis, scoliosis, fracture types
Nervous system roots: neur/o, encephal/o, myel/o, cerebr/o
Conditions: stroke (CVA), epilepsy, meningitis, neuropathy, concussion
Procedures: arthroplasty, laminectomy, EEG, EMG, lumbar puncture
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interpreting an orthopedic surgical report and a neurology consultation note

review.value: arthr/o (joint) + -scopy (visual examination) = minimally invasive joint examination and treatment using a scope
encephal/o (brain) + -itis (inflammation) = inflammation of the brain, often caused by viral infection

class_schedule.topic: Clinical Coding Systems: ICD-10, CPT, SNOMED CT
learning_objectives: Explain the purpose and structure of ICD-10-CM and ICD-10-PCS
Differentiate SNOMED CT from classification systems
readings: Medical Terminology: A Living Language Ch. 15

lecture_notes.introduction: Coding systems translate medical terminology into standardized codes for billing, quality reporting, and research. Understanding these systems is where medical terminology meets health informatics.
key_concepts: ICD-10-CM structure: 3-7 character alphanumeric codes for diagnoses
ICD-10-PCS: procedure coding with 7-character codes for inpatient procedures
CPT (Current Procedural Terminology): 5-digit codes for outpatient procedures
SNOMED CT: comprehensive clinical terminology with concept-based structure
Mapping between terminology systems for interoperability
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hands-on coding exercise: given a clinical scenario, assign ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes and CPT procedure codes

review.value: International Classification of Diseases, 10th Revision, Clinical Modification - used for diagnosis coding in all U.S. healthcare settings
Systematized Nomenclature of Medicine - Clinical Terms: a reference terminology with 350,000+ concepts for detailed clinical documentation

learning_objectives: Define common pharmacological terms and drug classifications
Understand prescription abbreviations and their risks
Explain how terminology supports medication safety systems
readings: Medical Terminology: A Living Language Ch. 14

lecture_notes.introduction: Medication errors are a leading cause of preventable harm. Precise pharmacological terminology and informatics-based safety systems are essential defenses.
key_concepts: Drug naming: chemical, generic, and brand names
Routes of administration: PO, IV, IM, SQ, SL, topical, transdermal
Prescription abbreviations: QD, BID, TID, QID, PRN, and dangerous abbreviations to avoid
Drug classifications: analgesics, antibiotics, anticoagulants, antihypertensives
RxNorm and NDC codes in informatics systems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interpreting a medication list from an EHR, identifying drug names, dosages, routes, and frequencies

review.value: NLM standard for normalized names of clinical drugs, linking brand and generic names across pharmacy and EHR systems
U (units), QD (daily), MS (morphine sulfate) - Joint Commission prohibited abbreviations due to confusion risk

quizzes.topic: Word Building and Body Systems Terminology

questions.question: Break down the following medical term into its components and define each part: 'electrocardiography'. Then construct a medical term meaning 'inflammation of the stomach and intestines'.
questions.answer: Electrocardiography: electr/o (electricity) + cardi/o (heart) + -graphy (process of recording) = the process of recording the electrical activity of the heart. Term for inflammation of the stomach and intestines: gastr/o (stomach) + enter/o (intestine) + -itis (inflammation) = gastroenteritis.

questions.question: A radiology report states: 'Transverse CT scan reveals a 2cm mass in the anterior mediastinum, superior to the cardiac silhouette, with lateral extension to the left hemithorax.' Translate all anatomical directional and positional terms into plain language.
questions.answer: Transverse: a horizontal cross-section (axial plane). Anterior mediastinum: the front compartment of the central chest. Superior to the cardiac silhouette: above the outline of the heart. Lateral extension: spreading toward the side. Left hemithorax: the left half of the chest cavity. Plain language: A horizontal CT image shows a 2cm mass in the front-center of the chest, above the heart, spreading to the left side of the chest.

rubric.key: Correct translation of each directional term

questions.question: Explain the difference between ICD-10-CM and SNOMED CT. When would a health informatics professional need to map between the two systems?
questions.answer: ICD-10-CM is a classification system with approximately 70,000 codes used primarily for billing and statistical reporting. SNOMED CT is a reference terminology with over 350,000 concepts designed for detailed clinical documentation. Mapping between them is needed when clinical data recorded in SNOMED CT in an EHR must be reported to payers or registries using ICD-10-CM codes, or when population health queries need to aggregate data documented at different granularity levels.

rubric.key: Correct characterization of ICD-10-CM purpose and scope
Correct characterization of SNOMED CT purpose and scope
Valid mapping use case with practical context

questions.question: Interpret the following medication order and identify any safety concerns: 'Metoprolol 50mg PO BID, Lisinopril 10mg PO QD, Warfarin 5mg PO QHS, Aspirin 81mg PO QD'
questions.answer: Interpretation: Metoprolol 50mg by mouth twice daily (beta-blocker for BP/heart rate), Lisinopril 10mg by mouth once daily (ACE inhibitor for BP), Warfarin 5mg by mouth at bedtime (anticoagulant), Aspirin 81mg by mouth once daily (antiplatelet). Safety concern: Warfarin + Aspirin together significantly increase bleeding risk. This combination requires careful monitoring of INR levels and clinical justification. The abbreviation QD is on the ISMP error-prone list and should be written as 'daily'.

rubric.key: Correct interpretation of all four medications

major_assignments.title: Clinical Documentation Terminology Analysis
major_assignments.description: Analyze a set of de-identified clinical documents (discharge summary, radiology report, pathology report, operative note) identifying all medical terms, categorizing them by body system, and mapping key diagnoses to ICD-10-CM and SNOMED CT codes.
objectives: Demonstrate mastery of medical terminology across body systems
Apply word-building principles to interpret unfamiliar terms
Practice mapping clinical terms to standardized coding systems
Understand how terminology precision impacts clinical documentation quality
deliverables: Terminology analysis spreadsheet with term, components, definition, and system classification
Coding exercise with ICD-10-CM and SNOMED CT mappings for 20 diagnoses
Written reflection (3-4 pages) on terminology challenges encountered
Presentation of most complex or interesting findings (10 minutes)

criteria: Accurate component breakdown for each term
Comprehensive identification and analysis of medical terminology
Accurate coding to ICD-10-CM and SNOMED CT
Insightful reflection connecting terminology to informatics

weekly_quizzes.description: Weekly terminology quizzes covering word building and body system vocabulary

class_participation.description: Active participation in terminology exercises and clinical note interpretation

major_assignments.description: Clinical documentation terminology analysis and coding project

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive exam on word building, body systems, and directional terminology

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including coding systems and pharmacology terminology

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected at all sessions. Vocabulary mastery requires consistent practice. More than 3 unexcused absences may result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 10% per day. Terminology quizzes cannot be made up without prior arrangement or documented emergency.
course_policies.collaboration: Study groups for vocabulary practice are encouraged. All graded work must be completed individually.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be original. Copying from coding references without demonstrating understanding will be treated as academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Internet access for ICD-10 and SNOMED CT browsers. Medical terminology flashcard app recommended (Quizlet or similar).
